skill.name: tavily-search-free
skill.description: Run Tavily-powered web searches for current research and RAG workflows using a small Python command-line script.
skill.summary: Search the web with Tavily and return LLM-friendly results for research and RAG tasks.
skill.author: Dwsy

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, network, filesystem, and credential patterns. Review confirms the executable code is a small Tavily search wrapper, but it sends user queries to an external API and reads TAVILY_API_KEY from the environment. No evidence found of prompt injection, hidden exfiltration, obfuscated payloads, or malicious command execution.

medium_findings.title: External Search API Receives User Queries
medium_findings.description: The script creates a TavilyClient and sends the provided query, search depth, and result limit to Tavily. This is expected for a search skill, but user prompts and research topics leave the local environment.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly passes the command-line query into client.search. The behavior matches the skill purpose, but it is still real outbound data transfer.
medium_findings.remediation: Warn users that queries are sent to Tavily and avoid submitting secrets or confidential project details.

medium_findings.title: Environment API Key Loading
medium_findings.description: The script loads dotenv configuration and reads TAVILY_API_KEY from the environment. This is normal credential handling, but it requires careful local secret management.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: There is direct evidence of dotenv loading and TAVILY_API_KEY access. No hardcoded secret value was found in the reviewed files.
medium_findings.remediation: Require users to provide their own key and keep .env files out of version control and shared logs.

medium_findings.title: User-Visible Shell Commands for Installation and Execution
medium_findings.description: The documentation instructs users to install dependencies and run the Python search script. These are visible setup commands, not hidden code execution inside the script.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit documentation examples with mostly fixed arguments. They still install third-party packages and execute a local script.
medium_findings.remediation: Publish with a warning that users should review package sources and run commands only in trusted environments.

low_findings.title: Documentation Example Writes Search Results to Disk
low_findings.description: An example script writes Tavily results to a timestamped file in the current working directory. This is a normal example, but saved search results may contain sensitive query context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file write is explicit and limited to a generated local filename. The risk is privacy and local data retention, not arbitrary filesystem access.
low_findings.remediation: Tell users to store results in approved locations and delete files that contain sensitive research topics.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Documentation Links
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL findings point to Tavily documentation, Tavily home pages, GitHub resources, or example output. They do not show hidden outbound destinations in code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited URLs appear in Markdown examples and resource lists. The executable script relies on the Tavily SDK rather than hardcoded exfiltration URLs.
low_findings.remediation: No code change is required for these documentation links.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto and Entropy Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The static weak cryptography and entropy alerts do not correspond to cryptographic operations or encoded payload execution in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found normal prose, argument parser text, and search examples at these locations. No hashing, encryption, or binary payload behavior was present.
low_findings.remediation: No remediation is needed for the dismissed crypto and entropy alerts.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credentialed External API Call
dangerous_patterns.description: The script combines an environment API key with an outbound Tavily search request. This pattern is expected but should be disclosed to users.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code reads TAVILY_API_KEY and uses it to initialize TavilyClient before making a search request. That confirms a credentialed network pattern.
dangerous_patterns.remediation: Document the outbound API behavior and credential requirement in the marketplace listing.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Dotenv Secret Loading
dangerous_patterns.description: The script loads environment variables from local dotenv configuration before reading TAVILY_API_KEY.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The import and load_dotenv call are visible in the script. This is legitimate but increases dependency on local secret hygiene.
dangerous_patterns.remediation: Advise users to keep dotenv files private and avoid shared workspaces for secrets.

content.value_statement: Current web research is slow when agents rely only on local context. This skill runs Tavily searches and returns concise results for RAG and research workflows.
actual_capabilities: Runs Tavily searches from a Python command-line script.
Supports basic and advanced search depth options.
Limits returned results with a max-results argument.
Reads TAVILY_API_KEY from environment or dotenv configuration.
Prints Tavily search responses for agent consumption.
limitations: Requires a valid Tavily API key before use.
Sends search queries to an external Tavily service.
Depends on tavily-python and python-dotenv packages.
Does not include result caching or source verification logic.

use_cases.title: Research Current Topics
use_cases.description: Find recent web sources for questions that need information beyond the model context.

use_cases.title: Feed RAG Pipelines
use_cases.description: Collect concise search results that can seed retrieval workflows and draft source lists.

use_cases.title: Compare Technical Options
use_cases.description: Search for recent comparisons, best practices, and documentation before making implementation decisions.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Web Search
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner search for a current topic.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Tavily search to find recent sources about remote work policy trends. Return the top five useful results.

prompt_templates.title: Focused Technical Search
prompt_templates.scenario: Find implementation guidance for a software task.
prompt_templates.prompt: Search for current Python async error handling best practices. Summarize the most actionable sources.

prompt_templates.title: Advanced Research Sweep
prompt_templates.scenario: Use deeper search for a complex topic.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use advanced Tavily search for autonomous agent evaluation methods. Return eight sources with short relevance notes.

prompt_templates.title: RAG Source Collection
prompt_templates.scenario: Gather source candidates for retrieval ingestion.
prompt_templates.prompt: Search for recent vector database benchmarking reports. Identify sources suitable for a RAG knowledge base.

output_examples.input: Search for latest AI agent framework comparisons.
output: A ranked list of relevant web pages.
Each result includes a title, URL, content summary, and relevance score.

output_examples.input: Find sources about Docker container permission errors.
output: Short summaries help the agent decide which result to inspect first.

output_examples.input: Search advanced results for fintech market trends.
output: Higher-depth search results for market research.
The response can be used to draft a source-backed briefing.

best_practices: Use specific search terms to improve result relevance.
Keep TAVILY_API_KEY in a private environment file or secret manager.
Review returned sources before using them in final answers.
anti_patterns: Do not send passwords, private keys, or confidential data as search queries.
Do not assume returned search summaries are verified facts.
Do not commit dotenv files or saved search outputs with sensitive content.

faq.question: Does this skill require an API key?
faq.answer: Yes. It reads TAVILY_API_KEY from the environment or a dotenv file.

faq.question: Does it access the network?
faq.answer: Yes. It sends search requests to Tavily through the Tavily Python SDK.

faq.question: Can it run advanced searches?
faq.answer: Yes. The command supports basic and advanced search depth values.

faq.question: Can it limit result count?
faq.answer: Yes. The max-results option controls the number of returned results.

faq.question: Does it verify source accuracy?
faq.answer: No. Users should inspect sources and confirm important claims independently.

faq.question: Is it safe for confidential research?
faq.answer: Use caution. Search queries are sent to Tavily, so avoid sensitive or private information.
